title: Introduction
description: NOC 63201 – Butchers - retail and wholesale | GTR Immigration Professional butchers under NOC 63201 are the backbone of Canada's robust food retail and wholesale sectors. Beyond simple meat cutting, these TEER 3 experts manage the preparation, aging, and presentation of high-quality proteins for a diverse consumer market. As a skilled trade, butchery is currently in high demand, particularly through Category-Based Selection in Express Entry (Trades category). For immigration, this role offers a secure and high-growth pathway to Permanent Residency (PR), as Canadian supermarkets and independent butcher shops continue to face significant labor shortages.
duties: Cut, trim, bone, and otherwise prepare standard and custom cuts of meat, poultry, fish, and shellfish.
Shape, lace, and tie roasts and other meats for display or delivery.
Manage inventory, keep records of sales, and determine product freshness and product lines.
Adhere to Hazard Analysis and Critical Control Points (HACCP) and Canadian Food Inspection Agency (CFIA) standards.
Ensure food storage conditions (refrigeration/freezer) are adequate and safe.
May supervise, direct work, or train other butchers, meat cutters, or fishmongers.
employment_requirements: Completion of secondary school is usually required.
Completion of a college or other butchery training program may be required.
Several years of on-the-job training in food stores is usually mandatory for retail butchers.
Knowledge of food handling, food safety, and sanitation standards is essential.
job_outlook: The outlook is Stable with Growth. According to the Canadian Occupational Projection System (COPS), this occupation is expected to face a Moderate Risk of Shortage through 2033. This means that job openings (estimated at 4,700 over the decade) will slightly outpace the number of new job seekers, ensuring strong placement opportunities for international candidates.
labour_market_demand: The demand for retail and wholesale butchers is currently rated as Favourable to Good across most Canadian provinces. With over 80% of the workforce employed in food and beverage stores, the industry is seeking international talent to replace retiring professionals and support the expansion of specialty and ethnic butcher shops. Major urban centers like Toronto, Vancouver, and Calgary are particularly active in recruiting skilled butchers with LMIA-supported offers.
In-Demand Skilled Trade: Butchers are prioritized in many federal and provincial immigration draws.
Technological Integration: Gain experience with modern machinery and high-standard safety protocols (HACCP/CFIA).
Entrepreneurial Potential: High success rate for those looking to eventually open their own specialized butcher or meat shop.
Work-Life Balance: Most retail butchery roles offer stable 35-40 hour work weeks with clear shifts.
pathways: Express Entry (Category-Based Selection): Targeted invitations for skilled trades like butchers.
Federal Skilled Trades Program (FSTP): A dedicated route for candidates with valid job offers or trade certification.
Provincial Nominee Program (PNP): In-demand streams in provinces like Saskatchewan, BC, and Ontario.
Canadian Experience Class (CEC): The fastest route for those with one year of local Canadian experience.
